title: File Formats, Parquet & DuckDB
text:
● Data Storage Essentials
    File Formats,Parquet &DuckDB
    How data is stored on disk shapes how fast you can query it along with the space it takes up on the disk. From row-based CSVs to columnar Parquet files to embedded analytical databases every format has benefits and trade-offs.
    
      Storage Layouts
      Parquet Internals
      DuckDB Architecture
      When to Use What
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    File formats & storage layouts
    Before writing a single query, the data has to live somewhere. How a file arranges its bytes on disk — row by row, column by column, or a hybrid of both — determines what kinds of queries run fast and what kinds run slow.
  





  

    
    
      
        Storage layout comparison
        

          
          
            Original Table
            
              
                Col A
                Col B
                Col C
                Col D
              
              
                A1B1C1D1
              
              
                A2B2C2D2
              
              
                A3B3C3D3
              
            
            3 rows × 4 columns. Is the logical table before any storage layout is applied.
          

          
          
             Row-Based (CSV, JSON)
            
              A1B1C1D1
              A2B2C2D2
              A3B3C3D3
            
            All columns per row stored together on disk
          

          
          
            Columnar (Pure Columnar)
            
              A1A2A3A4
              B1B2B3B4
              C1C2C3C4
              D1D2D3D4
            
            Each column stored contiguously — read only what you need
          

          
          
            Hybrid (Parquet) — Row Groups
            
              Row Group 1
              
                A1A2
                B1B2
                C1C2
                D1D2
              
              Row Group 2
              
                A3A4
                B3B4
                C3C4
                D3D4
              
            
            Rows chunked into groups; within each group, stored columnarly
          

        
      
    

    
    
      
        Format comparison
        



Feature
CSV
JSON
Parquet




Layout
Row
Row
Hybrid


Human-readable
Yes
Yes
No


Built-in compression
No
No
Yes


Schema embedded
No
Partial
Yes


Query efficiency
Low
Low
High


Typical file size
Medium
Large
Small


Best for
Simple exports
APIs / config
Analytics




      
    

    
    
      
        Relative file size — same dataset
        
          
            JSON
            
              1,834,260 bytes
            
          
          
            CSV
            
              829,667 bytes
            
          
          
            Parquet
            
              
  327,738 bytes

            
          
        
        
          Why is Parquet so much smaller?
          
            Columnar storage groups similar values together, making compression extremely effective. Parquet also uses dictionary encoding, replacing repeated values like "California" with a tiny integer code across all occurrences.
          
        
        
          
            Parquet
            1×
            baseline
          
          
            CSV
            2.5×
            larger
          
          
            JSON
            5.6×
            larger
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      1.1 · Storage Layouts
      How a file stores its bytes changes everything
      A main distinction between file storage tupes is how the data is grouped, mainly row by row or column by column. This different impacts what kinds of queries and tasks perform more efficiently.
      A row-based format like CSV or JSON stores all of a record's fields together. Reading one row is fast, but reading a single column across millions of rows means touching every row's data on disk, even the columns you don't care about.
      A columnar format stores all values from one column together. Analytical queries that compute aggregates over one or two columns out of dozens skip the irrelevant columns entirely, the disk reads are far smaller and thus more efficient.
      Parquet uses a hybrid: it partitions rows into large row groups (typically 100K–1M rows each) for large datasets, and within each group stores data columnarly. This gives columnar compression benefits while keeping related rows together for efficient scanning.
      
        Row-based
        Columnar
        Hybrid
        Row Groups
      
    

    
      1.2 · Formats Compared
      CSV, JSON, and Parquet serve different purposes
      CSV is the simplest and typically most standard file type. It is a plain-text file where every value is separated by a comma representing each row at a time. Anyone can open it in a spreadsheet and read it. But it has no schema (a formal record of column names and their data types), no type information, no compression, and the database has to infer everything.
      JSON is more expressive, it supports nested objects and arrays, making it ideal for APIs and configuration. But nesting means the structure is irregular, which makes bulk analysis slow and produces large files. JSON is the least efficient storage format for analytics and typically the largest in size.
      Parquet is purpose-built for analytical workloads and storing information efficiently (compactly). It is not human-readable, in binary format but it stores the full schema in its footer, supports built-in column-level compression, and enables query engines to skip entire row groups that can't match a filter using stored statistics (located in the footer of the file).
      
        CSV: simple & portable
        JSON: flexible & verbose
        Parquet: fast & compact
      
    

    
      1.3 · Compression & Encoding
      Columnar storage compresses information more efficiently
      The graphic on the left comes from a real experiment comparing the same dataset stored in all three formats. Each format stores the exact same information from the Stanford Open Policing Project dataset from Los Angeles. However, the difference is striking in file size: the Parquet file is over 5× smaller than JSON and nearly 2.5× smaller than CSV.
      This happens for two reasons. First, columnar grouping means similar values sit next to each other in the file,  compression algorithms like Snappy or Zstd exploit this repetition extremely well. Second, Parquet uses dictionary encoding: if a string column contains the same 50 city names repeated millions of times, Parquet replaces each string with a compact integer and stores a single dictionary. The savings compound to make a strong disparity.
      Smaller files mean fewer bytes read from disk, which is usually the primary bottleneck in data analysis. Parquet's compression is not just a storage benefit, it is a query performance benefit too.
      
        Dictionary encoding
        Snappy / Zstd compression
        Less disk I/O
        Schema in footer
      
    

  




  
    Fun historical insight
    Parquet was actually made by Twitter, and Google aided in its creation.
    Parquet's origin story actually traces back to a 2010 Google research paper called "Dremel: Interactive Analysis of Web-Scale Datasets", which introduced the idea of representing nested data in a columnar format. This trick let Google query massive datasets interactively instead of waiting on slow batch jobs.Three years later, in 2013, two unlikely co-conspirators Twitter and Cloudera joined forces and built Parquet together and donated it to the Apache Software Foundation, making it free and open for anyone to use. Twitter needed to crunch enormous volumes of data fast, and Cloudera was deep in the business of helping companies do exactly that at scale. Today Parquet is widely known as a open source file storage format purpose-built for analytical OLAP-style workloads, heavy on reads and aggregations, over huge piles of data, and it's remarkably space-efficient.
  




  02
  
    Deep Dive
    Inside a Parquet file
    Parquet's speed comes from its internal structure. Understanding how a Parquet file is laid out on disk explains why query engines can skip irrelevant data without reading it.
  





  

    
    
      
        Internal layout of a .parquet file
        
          PAR1  (magic bytes — identifies Parquet format)

          
          
            Row Group 1  (e.g. rows 0 – 999,999)
            
              
                Col A
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
                Data Page 2
              
              
                Col B
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
                Data Page 2
              
              
                Col C
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
                Data Page 2
              
            
          

          
          
            Row Group 2  (e.g. rows 1,000,000 – 1,999,999)
            
              
                Col A
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
              
              
                Col B
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
              
              
                Col C
                Dict Page
                Data Page 1
              
            
            
            Dict Page: stores the lookup table of unique values. Like a dictionary that translates the actual data to simpler forms like integers if not already an integer.
            Data Page: a group of data entries, stores the actual column values (as integer codes if dict-encoded)
          
          

          
            FOOTER — schema, row group byte offsets, statistics (min/max per column per row group), encodings
          
          PAR1  (magic bytes — end of file)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Row group skipping in action

        Query: WHERE date > '2023-06-01'

        
        
          
          
            
              Row Group 1
              date: 2021-01-01 → 2021-12-31
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
            
              Row Group 2
              date: 2022-01-01 → 2022-12-31
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
            
              Row Group 3
              date: 2023-01-01 → 2023-05-30
            
            ⏭ SKIPPED
          
          
          
            
              Row Group 4
              date: 2023-06-01 → 2023-12-31
            
            ✓ READ
          
          
            
              Row Group 5
              date: 2024-01-01 → 2024-12-31
            
            ✓ READ
          
        

        
          The footer's per-row-group min/max statistics let the engine skip 3 of 5 row groups before reading a single data byte.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Parquet advantages
        

          
            
            
              Columnar grouped storage
              Reads only the columns needed: analytical queries touch a fraction of the data
            
          

          
            
            
              Compression efficiency
              Built-in Snappy / Zstd compression: Parquet files are consistently 3–10× smaller than equivalent CSVs
            
          

          
            
            
              Dictionary encoding
              Replaces repeated string values with compact integer codes: huge savings on categorical columns
            
          

          
            
            
              Schema embedded in footer
              Column names, types, and statistics are stored in the file itself: no external schema needed
            
          

          
            
            
              Schema evolution
              Add or remove columns without rewriting the entire file: new tools can simply ignore unknown columns
            
          

        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · File Structure
      A Parquet file has more than just data
      Every Parquet file starts and ends with the four magic bytes PAR1. This simply identify's the file on the computer as a Parquet file. Between the magic bytes, the data is divided into row groups: horizontal partitions, each containing a chunk of rows. Within each row group, every column's data is stored as its own contiguous column chunk, further split into pages. Pages are the smallest unit of storage in Parquet. Each page holds a batch of values for one column, and a column chunk is simply a sequence of these pages read one after another.
      The footer at the end of the file is critical and unique to parquet files compared to JSON or CSV. It holds the full schema (column names and data types), the byte offset (the exact position in the file, measured in bytes from the start, where each row group begins) of every row group, and per-column statistics including minimum and maximum values. A query engine reads the footer first and uses it to build a plan before reading any actual data. 
      The footer is created automatically when the Parquet file is written. The software writing the file (such as DuckDB, Spark, or the R arrow package) tracks the schema, byte offsets, and min/max statistics as it writes each row group, then writes the metadata into the footer as the very last step before closing the file.
      This design means a tool like DuckDB can open a Parquet file, read the footer in milliseconds, and immediately know which row groups can be skipped for a given query filter,without touching any data pages.
      
        PAR1 magic bytes
        Row groups
        Column chunks
        Footer metadata
      
    

    
      2.2 · Predicate Pushdown
      Row group skipping and why it is beneficial
      Parquet's footer stores the minimum and maximum value of every column in every row group, which applies to any data type. This enables a technique called predicate pushdown: pushing a filter condition down into the storage layer so data that can't possibly match is never loaded.
      Consider WHERE date > '2023-06-01' against a file with five row groups. The footer reveals row groups 1–3 have max dates before the cutoff. The engine skips them entirely. Only groups 4 and 5 are read. If you have 100 row groups and the filter eliminates 90, you read 10% of the file.
      This is a form of metadata-driven pruning. It doesn't require a B+ tree index since the statistics in the footer are sufficient. It is one of the main reasons Parquet files can be queried efficiently by tools that don't load the data into a database first.
      
        Predicate pushdown
        Min/max statistics
        Row group pruning
        No index needed
      
    

    
      2.3 · When to Use Parquet
      Parquet Benefits
      Use Parquet when your workflow is analytical. In other words, when your frequent queries aggregate, filter, or summarize across many rows. It outperforms CSV and JSON on these workloads not by a small margin but by an order of magnitude, because it reads far fewer bytes from disk.
      Parquet's universality is also a major advantage. It works seamlessly across the entire modern data stack — Spark, DuckDB, Pandas, Polars, BigQuery, Snowflake, Athena, and Hive all read and write Parquet natively, as do R packages like arrow and dplyr via dbplyr. Choosing Parquet as your storage format means you can utilize many various tools.
      The only reason to prefer CSV or JSON over Parquet is human readability and editability. If someone needs to open the file in a text editor or spreadsheet, CSV would be better. If you're building an API response payload, JSON would be better. If you're storing data for analysis, Parquet is almost always the better choice.
      
        Analytical workloads
        Universal compatibility
        Schema evolution
        Not human-readable
      
    

  




  
    So who actually queries Parquet?
    DuckDB, known as the "SQLite for analytics" is a full SQL engine that runs entirely inside your process, with no server to configure.
    DuckDB can query Parquet files directly on disk with no load step. It combines Parquet's columnar storage efficiency with full SQL expressiveness and the simplicity of an embedded database.
  




  03
  
    Embedded Analytics
    DuckDB
    DuckDB is an open-source, in-process analytical SQL database. It brings the full capabilities of SQL to local data files — CSV, Parquet, JSON — without requiring a server, users, passwords, or any infrastructure.
  





  

    
    
      
        DuckDB — how it fits in your workflow
        
          
            
            
              Your Program (Python / R / CLI)
              You write SQL queries and receive results as data frames or tables
            
          
          ↕ in-process (no network hop)
          
            
            
              DuckDB Engine
              SQL parser → optimizer → vectorized execution engine
            
          
          ↕ reads directly
          
            
            
              Local Files
              Can read parquet files directly, no loading required
            
          
          — or writes to —
          
            
            
              DuckDB Database (.duckdb)
              Single file on disk. Persistent, columnar, compressed storage
            
          
        
        
          No server. No users. No config. Install as a package and start querying.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        DuckDB vs. MySQL vs. PostgreSQL
        
        



Property
MySQL
PostgreSQL
DuckDB




Architecture
Client-server
Client-server
In-process


Optimized for
OLTP
OLTP + OLAP
OLAP


Setup required
Server + config
Server + config
pip/install.packages


Storage layout
Row (B+ tree)
Row (heap)
Columnar


Query files directly
No
No
Yes (CSV/Parquet/JSON)


Best for
Web apps
Complex apps
Local analytics




        
          DuckDB is not a replacement for MySQL or PostgreSQL. It's a different tool for a different job: local analytical queries instead of multi-user transactional applications.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Querying files directly — no database needed
        
-- Query a Parquet file directly on disk
SELECT
  column_a,
  SUM(column_b) AS total
FROM read_parquet('data.parquet')
WHERE column_a = 'value'
GROUP BY column_a;
-- Query ALL parquet files in a folder
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('folder/*.parquet');
-- Load into a persistent DuckDB database
CREATE TABLE stops AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('*.parquet');
        
        
          DuckDB reads the Parquet footer first, pushes predicates into the scan, and skips irrelevant row groups.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · What is DuckDB
      An embedded SQL engine and no server required
      DuckDB is described as "the SQLite for analytics." SQLite is the local SQL dialect and like SQLite, DuckDB is an embedded, in-process database. It runs entirely inside your program rather than as a separate server. The entire engine is a single library you install as a package in Python or R, though DuckDB also ships a standalone CLI (command-line interface) you can run directly from the terminal without any host language. There is no authentication, no port to configure, and no connection to start.
      Unlike SQLite, which is optimized for transactional row-level operations just like MySQL, DuckDB is purpose-built for analytical workloads. It uses a columnar execution engine, processes data in batches, and is optimized for queries that scan many rows, aggregate, group, filter, and join large datasets.
      A DuckDB database is a single file on disk with a .duckdb extension. You can also use DuckDB in fully in-memory mode, where nothing is written to disk at all. Both modes give you full SQL expressiveness with analytical performance.
      
        Embedded / in-process
        No server needed
        Columnar execution
        Single-file database
      
    

    
      3.2 · DuckDB vs. Traditional SQL
      A different kind of SQL database
      MySQL and PostgreSQL are client-server databases: a long-running server process manages shared access, handles concurrent transactions, and stores data in row-oriented storage. This architecture is excellent for web applications with many simultaneous users reading and writing small numbers of rows.
      DuckDB makes the opposite trade-offs. It is designed for a single analyst or process running complex queries over large datasets. Because it runs in-process, there is no network overhead between the query engine and the query. The results come back as in-memory data frames, not network packets like with MySQL or PostgreSQL.
      The storage engine is also fundamentally different. MySQL stores table data in B+ trees (row-oriented). DuckDB stores data columnarly with vectorized execution, processing columns in batches of 2,048 values at a time. This is precisely what makes aggregation queries fast: the CPU can operate on contiguous arrays of values rather than jumping between rows.
      
        Vectorized execution
        No network overhead
        Batch processing
        Single-user analytics
      
    

    
      3.3 · Querying Files Directly
      DuckDB can query Parquet files without loading them first
      One of DuckDB's most powerful features is the ability to run SQL queries directly against parquet files on disk, no database ingestion step required. The read_parquet() function accepts a file path (or a glob pattern like *.parquet) and exposes it as a queryable table.
      This is not just a convenience feature. DuckDB's Parquet reader exploits the file's footer statistics to implement predicate pushdown, the same row group skipping described earlier. A filter in your SQL WHERE clause is pushed all the way into the file scan, skipping row groups that can't match. This means you get analytical warehouse behavior on local files.
      When you do want persistence, the CREATE TABLE AS SELECT pattern copies data into DuckDB's own columnar format, which is even faster for repeated queries. The choice between querying files directly and loading into a native database is a trade-off depending on the query and the frequency of the each type of querying.
      
        read_parquet()
        Glob patterns
        Predicate pushdown
        CTAS for persistence
      
    

  






  Key Insights
  
    
      01
      Storage layout determines query performance. Row-based formats (CSV, JSON) store each record together: good for returning full rows, slow for scanning one column across millions. Columnar formats store each column together: excellent for analytical aggregations, less ideal for full-row retrieval.
    
    
      02
      Parquet is a hybrid: rows are partitioned into large row groups, and within each group data is stored columnarly. This gives both compression efficiency and fast columnar scans. The footer stores schema and per-row-group statistics enabling predicate pushdown: row groups are skipped before any data is read.
    
    
      03
      Parquet compresses dramatically better than CSV or JSON due to columnar grouping (similar values together) and dictionary encoding (repeating strings replaced with compact integer codes). The same dataset can be 3–10× smaller as Parquet, which means faster queries because less data is read from disk.
    
    
      04
      DuckDB is an in-process analytical SQL database, install as a package, query immediately. A database is a single .duckdb file. It uses vectorized, columnar execution and processes data in batches of 2,048 values, making it fast for aggregation-heavy analytical queries.
    
    
      05
      Once ingested into DuckDB, the source format is irrelevant. DuckDB normalizes CSV, Parquet, and JSON into its own internal columnar representation. Query performance converges regardless of source. Parquet is still the best source format because it ingests information faster and the file is smaller on disk.
    
    
      06
      Native DuckDB and direct Parquet are both columnar but not the same. Parquet is a file format read on demand from disk. DuckDB ingests data into its own internal columnar storage. Once inside, the source format no longer matters. The trade-off is storage (DuckDB takes ~3× more space) versus speed (DuckDB is faster for repeated queries). Choose direct Parquet for one-off queries; choose native DuckDB when querying the same data frequently.

title: sql4stats
text: Welcome to the SQL for Statistics site! This site is designed to help you learn SQL in the context of statistical analysis. Here, you’ll find tutorials, examples, and resources that will guide you through using SQL to manipulate and analyze data effectively. Whether you’re a beginner or looking to enhance your SQL skills for statistical applications, this site has something for you.
